## Introduction to Mesozeaxanthin  

Mesozeaxanthin (3R,3′S-β,β-carotene-3,3′-diol) is a non-provitamin A xanthophyll, a subclass of terpenoids characterized by oxygen-containing functional groups (hydroxyls in this case) and a C40 isoprenoid backbone. Structurally, it is a diastereomer of lutein (3R,3′R) and zeaxanthin (3R,3′R)—the other major macular xanthophylls—but is distinguished by its unique 3′-S chiral center, which arises from enzymatic or non-enzymatic isomerization of dietary precursors (Bone et al., 2001). Unlike lutein and zeaxanthin, mesozeaxanthin is not abundant in most plant-based foods; instead, it is primarily synthesized endogenously in the human retina via the isomerization of lutein, a process catalyzed by retinal pigment epithelial (RPE) cells (Bernstein et al., 2016). Small amounts have also been detected in certain seafood (e.g., salmon) and egg yolks, but these are negligible compared to endogenous production (Khachik et al., 2002).  

The biological significance of mesozeaxanthin is tightly linked to its role in the macula lutea, the central region of the retina responsible for high-acuity vision. Alongside lutein and zeaxanthin, mesozeaxanthin accumulates in the macular pigment (MP), a yellowish layer that absorbs short-wavelength (blue) light and scavenges reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated by phototransduction. Notably, mesozeaxanthin is the most concentrated xanthophyll in the foveal center (the region of sharpest vision), accounting for ~25–35% of total MP, compared to ~60% for zeaxanthin and ~10% for lutein (Bone et al., 2001). This spatial distribution suggests a specialized function: in vitro studies have shown that mesozeaxanthin exhibits greater antioxidant activity than lutein or zeaxanthin, particularly in neutralizing singlet oxygen and peroxyl radicals—key mediators of retinal oxidative stress (Beatty et al., 2000). Additionally, its molecular structure allows it to stabilize lipid membranes in the photoreceptor outer segments more effectively, reducing lipid peroxidation and maintaining membrane integrity under oxidative stress (Bernstein et al., 2016).  

Clinical research has explored the relationship between mesozeaxanthin and age-related macular degeneration (AMD), the leading cause of irreversible blindness in adults over 50. Low MP density is a well-established risk factor for AMD, and supplementation with mesozeaxanthin (in combination with lutein and zeaxanthin) has been shown to increase MP density in individuals with AMD or low baseline MP (Stringham & Hammond, 2005). A randomized controlled trial by Nolan et al. (2011) found that supplementation with a combination of 10 mg lutein, 10 mg zeaxanthin, and 10 mg mesozeaxanthin for 12 months significantly improved MP density and visual function (e.g., contrast sensitivity) in patients with early AMD, compared to a control group receiving only lutein and zeaxanthin. These effects are attributed to mesozeaxanthin’s enhanced ability to replenish foveal MP, as its endogenous synthesis may be insufficient in individuals with AMD or dietary lutein deficiency (Bernstein et al., 2016).  

Despite these findings, gaps in knowledge remain. For example, the exact mechanism of lutein isomerization to mesozeaxanthin in the RPE is not fully elucidated, and the long-term safety of high-dose mesozeaxanthin supplementation has not been rigorously evaluated. Furthermore, while observational studies link higher MP density to reduced AMD risk, causal evidence for mesozeaxanthin’s protective role requires larger, long-term trials (Beatty et al., 2000).  

In summary, mesozeaxanthin is a critical component of the macular pigment with unique structural and functional properties that support retinal health. Its concentration in the foveal center, potent antioxidant activity, and potential therapeutic value in AMD make it a key target for ongoing research into ocular nutrition and degenerative eye diseases.
